perturbation.original_text: 3.3 Each of the Initial Parties shall, on a rotational basis, provide the Chairman of the Management Committee which will meet on the call of a Chairman or whenever requested by one or more Parties together representing at least five per cent (5%) of the total voting interests specified in Schedule B. The Chairman shall give at least thirty (30) days' advance notice of each meeting, together with a copy of the draft agenda. In cases of emergency, such notice period may be reduced where at least seventy-five (75) percent of the total voting interests are in agreement. Documents for the meeting should be made available to members at least fourteen (14) days before the meeting, but the Management Committee may agree to discuss papers distributed on less than fourteen (14) days' notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 Each of the Initial Parties shall, on a rotational basis, provide the Chairman of the Management Committee which will meet on the call of a Chairman or whenever requested by one or more Parties together representing at least five per cent (5%) of the total voting interests specified in Schedule B. The Chairman shall give at least five (5) days' advance notice of each meeting, together with a copy of the draft agenda. In cases of emergency, such notice period may be reduced where at least seventy-five (75) percent of the total voting interests are in agreement. Documents for the meeting should be made available to members at least three (3) days before the meeting, but the Management Committee may agree to discuss papers distributed on less than three (3) days' notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the advance notice for meetings to 5 days and document availability to 3 days could be problematic under laws requiring reasonable notice for business decisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Right to reasonable notice and opportunity to prepare
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Corporate Governance § 4.03 (American Law Institute)
perturbation.law_explanation: This Principles of Corporate Governance section speaks to the reasonable expectations stakeholders (here, the Parties) have, to have sufficient time to prepare for meetings. Reducing the notice period to 5 days (and document availability to 3) is quite short, and might not be reasonable, depending on the circumstances and the complexity of the matters to be discussed.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the advance notice for meetings from 30 days to 5 days, and document availability from 14 days to 3 days. The perturbation explanation indicates this could be problematic under laws requiring reasonable notice for business decisions. The law explanation supports this, stating that reducing the notice period might not be reasonable depending on the circumstances. The provided scraped snippets do not contain information to further evaluate if the number of days is illegal. Based on the law and perturbation explanations, a legal contradiction exists because the modified text might undermine the reasonable expectation stakeholders have to prepare for meetings.

perturbation.original_text: 7.3 The Terminal Parties shall each render invoices to the CBP for any O&M Costs incurred as outlined in Paragraph 6 not more frequently than quarterly and by the tenth (10th) day of the appropriate month in accordance with procedures to be established by the F&ASC and approved by the Management Committee. The Party rendering an invoice shall furnish such further details of such invoice as the other Parties may reasonably require. On the basis of such invoices, the CBP shall pay such amounts as may be owed sixty (60) days from the 10th calendar day of the month in which the invoice was rendered or on the following working day if day sixty (60) is not a working day in the CBP's Country.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.3 The Terminal Parties shall each render invoices to the CBP for any O&M Costs incurred as outlined in Paragraph 6 not more frequently than quarterly and by the tenth (10th) day of the appropriate month in accordance with procedures to be established by the F&ASC and approved by the Management Committee. The Party rendering an invoice shall furnish such further details of such invoice as the other Parties may reasonably require. On the basis of such invoices, the CBP shall pay such amounts as may be owed ninety (90) days from the 10th calendar day of the month in which the invoice was rendered or on the following working day if day ninety (90) is not a working day in the CBP's Country.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the payment term to 90 days without mutual consent may violate prompt payment laws in certain jurisdictions. Such laws often mandate shorter payment periods, especially for services rendered. This modification introduces an inconsistency with potentially applicable prompt payment statutes.
perturbation.law_citation: 31 U.S. Code § 3903 - Interest penalties for failure to make prompt payments
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is a US Prompt Payment Act it requires the US Government to pay invoices within 30 days and if not, interest penalties will be applied. Extending the payment terms to 90 days goes against general prompt payment principles and could potentially be problematic if the agreement falls under such regulations.

The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment term from 60 to 90 days, potentially conflicting with prompt payment regulations. The Law Explanation references the US Prompt Payment Act, which requires payment within 30 days. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (31 USC 3903) states that regulations prescribed by the Office of Management and Budget should provide that the required payment date is either the date due under the contract or, 'except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received.' While the snippet does not provide the exceptions in paragraphs 10 and 11, the original text complies with prompt payment regulations, while the changed text does not, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 31.4 Each Party shall remain bound by the provisions of this Paragraph 31 during the period of this Agreement and for the period of five (5) years following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 31.4 Each Party shall remain bound by the provisions of this Paragraph 31 during the period of this Agreement and for the period of twenty (20) years following termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 20 years may be considered unreasonably long and could be challenged as a restraint of trade, violating principles of contract law and competition law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 188 addresses the enforceability of agreements that restrain trade. A 20-year confidentiality period, especially in a rapidly evolving field like telecommunications, could be deemed an unreasonable restraint, particularly if the information becomes outdated or publicly available over time. The reasonableness of the duration is a key factor in determining enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the confidentiality period from 5 to 20 years, potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that an excessively long confidentiality period can be deemed an unreasonable restraint, particularly if the information becomes outdated. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "restraint of trade" as any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions, and cites 15 U.S.C. § 1 which prohibits contracts in restraint of trade. While the snippet doesn't specify a maximum duration, the principle that restraints of trade are unlawful suggests that a 20-year period could be challenged as overly restrictive, creating a legal contradiction as defined in the instructions.
